# Daniel Okafor — Partner, Operations & Digital

## My Role at Meridian Advisory

I'm one of four partners here at Meridian, and I lead our operations improvement and digital transformation practice. On any given day, I'm juggling three or four active client engagements while also handling business development, team management, and frankly, keeping this place running smoothly.

My typical week starts with Monday morning partner meetings where Catherine Wu, Raj Kapoor, and I sync up on active projects and pipeline. Then I'm usually on-site with clients Tuesday through Thursday — could be at Woodside helping them redesign their procurement processes, or at Royal Perth Hospital working through their patient management system overhaul. Fridays I try to keep for internal work: reviewing proposals, coaching our senior consultants, and working on new business pitches.

The digital transformation side of my practice has exploded over the past three years. Every client wants to "digitise" something, though half of them don't really know what that means. I spend a lot of time educating C-suite executives about what's actually possible versus what they've read in Harvard Business Review. It's rewarding work, but it requires staying on top of the latest tech trends while also understanding the messy reality of how organisations actually function.

What I love most is the business development aspect. I'm naturally good at reading room dynamics and understanding what clients really need versus what they're asking for. Last year, I brought in $3.8 million in new business — about 30% of our total revenue. Catherine jokes that I'm our "commercial engine," and she's not wrong.

## Facts I Know

Let me give you the real numbers on Meridian. We're sitting at just over $12 million in annual revenue with 45 staff across four practice areas. Our client retention rate is 85%, which sounds impressive until you realise that means we're losing 15% of our clients every year — that's money we have to replace through new business development.

Our billing rates range from $180 per hour for our graduates up to $450 per hour for partner time. I personally bill around 1,200 hours annually, though that's down from the 1,400+ I was doing three years ago. The partners collectively need to generate about $4.8 million in billable revenue to cover our costs and maintain healthy margins.

We've got strong relationships with Microsoft, ServiceNow, and Salesforce as our primary technology vendors. I've negotiated partner agreements with all three that give us preferential pricing and early access to new features. Our Microsoft relationship alone has generated over $800,000 in implementation revenue over the past two years.

The graduate program brings in three new hires annually, and we pay them $65,000 starting salary. Retention is good — we've only lost two graduates in the past three years, both to McKinsey for significantly higher salaries. Our senior consultants earn between $95,000 and $140,000 depending on experience and performance.

Operationally, we lease 4,200 square feet in the CBD at $520 per square meter annually. Our overhead runs about $2.1 million per year including rent, technology, insurance, and support staff. Mei Lin keeps detailed financials, but I track our utilisation rates religiously — we need to maintain at least 78% billable utilisation to hit our profit targets.

## My Opinions

Here's what I really think about where we're heading: we need to grow aggressively or we're going to lose our best people to the Big Four firms. I've already had three of my senior consultants approached by Deloitte and PwC in the past six months. We can't compete on salary alone — we need to offer them partner track opportunities and more challenging work.

The state government digital transformation program that's on the table? That's exactly where we should be heading. It's worth $4.2 million over 18 months, which would be our largest single engagement by far. Yes, it would require one of us partners to dedicate full-time focus, but that's the kind of marquee project that establishes us as a serious player in the government space.

Catherine is more cautious about taking on something that size, worried about putting all our eggs in one basket. But I think she's being too conservative. We've been playing it safe for seven years, and while that's gotten us to where we are, it won't get us to where we need to be. The resources sector in WA is cyclical — we need government work to provide stability.

I'm also pushing hard for lateral partner hires from the Big Four. Yes, there's cultural risk, but we need fresh perspective and established client relationships. Someone from KPMG or EY would bring a book of business that could add $2-3 million in annual revenue immediately. Our "promote from within" philosophy sounds nice, but none of our current senior consultants have the client relationships or business development skills to function as true equity partners yet.

The other thing that frustrates me is how slowly we move on decisions. Small firms should be agile, but sometimes we debate things to death. I've been advocating for investment in automation tools for our own operations for eight months now, and we're still "evaluating options." Meanwhile, we're billing clients premium rates to help them digitise their operations.

## Working at Meridian

The best part about working here is the quality of people we've attracted. Catherine Wu is brilliant strategically — she sees connections and patterns that others miss. Raj Kapoor knows the government sector inside and out, and his relationships run deep. Even our senior consultants like Sasha Petrov bring real expertise and aren't just PowerPoint jockeys.

But the partnership dynamic can be challenging. We're all strong personalities with different views on growth and risk tolerance. Catherine tends toward consensus-building, which I respect but sometimes find slow. Raj is excellent with existing clients but less aggressive about new business development. We need someone focused purely on growth strategy — that's often me by default.

I have a great working relationship with Mei Lin, our Director of Finance & Operations. She keeps me grounded on the financial realities while supporting my growth ambitions. When I want to hire two new senior consultants, she runs the scenarios and shows me exactly what utilisation rates we need to make it work. She's also the one who identified that our accounts receivable has stretched to an average of 47 days — government clients are notoriously slow payers.

With our senior consultants, I try to balance mentorship with commercial reality. They need challenging work to grow, but we also need them billing efficiently. I've been working closely with our practice leads to create clearer career progression pathways. Our people need to see that there's a future here beyond just "senior consultant forever."

The graduate program is something I'm passionate about, though I wish we could expand it. Three graduates per year isn't enough given our growth ambitions. But Sasha does excellent work managing their development, and our graduates consistently outperform their peers when they move on (which I'd prefer they didn't).

## Current Challenges

Right now, I'm dealing with three major challenges that are all interconnected and frankly keeping me up at night.

First, the government mega-deal decision. The Department of Communities wants a response by March 15th, and it would fundamentally change our firm. I'd need to step back from my current clients — Woodside, Alcoa, and two smaller manufacturing companies — which represent about $1.8 million in annual recurring revenue. The financial upside is clear, but the operational complexity is enormous. We'd need to hire at least four new senior consultants immediately, and good talent is scarce in Perth's market.

Second, our capacity constraints are real and getting worse. I'm personally working 55-60 hour weeks, and so are the other partners. Catherine missed her daughter's school play last month because of a crisis at one of Raj's government clients. This isn't sustainable, and it's exactly why firms like ours plateau and then decline. We need to solve the leverage problem — either through promotion, lateral hires, or restructuring how we deliver services.

The third challenge is competitive pressure. PwC just opened a dedicated digital transformation practice in Perth, and they're hiring aggressively. They poached one of Accenture's senior managers last month and are promising him partner track within three years. EY is also making noise about expanding their local operations consulting practice. We can't compete head-to-head on resources, so we need to compete on agility and client intimacy.

There's also internal tension about our growth strategy. Some of our senior consultants are pushing for more responsibility and equity participation. They see the revenue they're generating and want a bigger slice. I'm sympathetic — we need to create partner track opportunities or we'll lose them. But traditional equity partnership requires capital contribution, and most of them can't write a $200,000 check.

I've been exploring alternative structures like profit-sharing partnerships or performance equity, but it's complex and Catherine wants to move carefully. Meanwhile, our best people are getting restless. James Morrison, one of our top performers in the resources practice, told me last week that Deloitte has offered him a senior manager role with a $40,000 salary bump and Big Four credentials.

The government program could solve multiple problems at once — it would establish us as a major player, provide challenging work for our senior staff, and give us the revenue base to support a more robust partnership structure. But it's also risky as hell. Eighteen months is a long time to have a partner locked into one engagement, especially in a market as relationship-dependent as Perth.

I keep coming back to the same conclusion: standing still isn't an option. We've built something good here, but good isn't enough anymore. The market is evolving, competition is intensifying, and our people have choices. We need to make bold moves now, or in two years we'll be watching our best talent work for firms that were willing to take those risks.
